chapter_summary: In the Naimisha forest, the sage Shaunaka asks the suta Ugrashravas (son of Lomaharshana) to recount the lineage of the Bhargavas (the line of Bhrigu), which his father had mastered. The suta gives the genealogy: from Bhrigu was born Chyavana; from Chyavana, the righteous Pramati; from Pramati, by the apsara Ghritachi, was born Ruru; and from Ruru, by Pramadvara, was born Shunaka, master of the Vedas and Shaunaka's own forefather. Shaunaka then asks why Chyavana bears that name. The suta explains with a tale: Bhrigu's devoted wife Puloma was pregnant by him. While Bhrigu was away for his ablutions, a rakshasa entered the hermitage, was seized with desire on seeing the faultless Puloma, and resolved to carry her off. He questioned the blazing fire-god (Jatavedas / Pavaka), who dwells within all beings as witness to good and evil, demanding the truth of whose wife she was, claiming he had betrothed her first before Bhrigu took her by deceit. The fire-god, hearing this, became deeply distressed, afraid both of speaking untruth and of Bhrigu's curse. The chapter breaks off as Agni, cornered between honesty and fear, prepares to answer.

story.title: Why Chyavana Is Called Chyavana: The Rakshasa, Puloma, and the Witnessing Fire
story.arc: A genealogy request opens into an origin-of-a-name tale. Shaunaka asks for the Bhargava line and then for the meaning of Chyavana's name. The suta answers by going back to before Chyavana's birth: his mother Puloma, pregnant with him, is left alone when Bhrigu departs for his ablutions. A rakshasa intrudes, lusts after her, and tries to extract from the ever-present fire-god a verdict that she is rightfully his — claiming a prior betrothal that Bhrigu allegedly overrode by deceit. The fire-god, sworn to truth and dwelling inside all creatures as the witness of every deed, is trapped between lying and incurring Bhrigu's wrath. The chapter ends on that knife-edge of distress, the answer withheld.
story.timeline_note: Frame is the Naimisha-forest recital by the suta to Shaunaka; the embedded tale is set generations earlier, before Chyavana's birth, explaining a name whose bearer already appears in the genealogy just given.
story.comic_potential: A cornered fire-god put on the spot as a cosmic lie-detector — forced to referee a demon's possessive marriage claim while quietly terrified of an absent sage's curse — is rich for wry, high-stakes-meets-awkward staging.

teachings.teaching: Fire (Agni / Jatavedas) moves within all beings as the inner witness of good and evil, present at all times; nothing done is unseen by it.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework the inner fire is the witnessing consciousness that pervades the practitioner: the sadhaka's every act is registered by the awareness seated within, so truth is not a social rule but the unavoidable testimony of the indwelling light.

teachings.teaching: Truthfulness is binding even on a god: Agni, being himself truth, is compelled to speak the truth when adjured by it, and dreads the guilt of untruth as much as the curse it would invite.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads satya as a structural law of consciousness, not mere ethics — even the luminous power within cannot evade it; alignment with truth is the condition of the inner fire's integrity.

teachings.teaching: Desire (hrcchaya, the heart-born one) overtakes the rakshasa on sight of Puloma and deranges his judgment, driving him to seize what is not his.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's reading, kama arising at the sense-gate clouds discrimination and converts a being into the agent of his own downfall; the disturbed mind grasps, and the grasping is itself the beginning of the fall the name 'Chyavana' (the fallen/slipped) will encode.

teachings.teaching: The recitation of sacred lineage is itself a sanctified, purpose-bearing act: the Bhargava line is one honored by the gods with Indra, Agni, and the Maruts, and recounting it is a worthy transmission from teacher to seeker.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma, lineage (parampara) is the living channel of transmission; to receive and pass on the line is to participate in the same continuity of grace that sustains the practice.
